note: Module 3 — who is already moving, and whether it crowds or validates. The analytical payoff is the layer split: regional front-end is concentrating in Singapore; the other five countries remain back-end / IDM. Items below verified against primary/company sources (July 2026).

reads_as_legend.validating: Confirms real demand / a viable regional layer.
reads_as_legend.crowding: Adds competing capacity that could compress returns.
reads_as_legend.context: Ecosystem or adjacency signal, not a direct competitive move.

players.move: US$4B expansion fab officially opened Sep 2023; adds ~450,000 300mm wafers/yr, taking GF Singapore to ~1.5M 300mm wafers/yr; ~1,000 jobs.
players.read_note: Direct peer confirming Singapore = the regional front-end anchor for specialty nodes. The clearest signal that front-end demand in the region concentrates in Singapore, not the five.

players.move: US$7.8B 300mm JV; 130–40nm mixed-signal, power-management and analog (automotive/industrial/consumer/mobile); 7 process licenses from TSMC. Construction began 2H 2024; first customer output 2027; ~55,000 wpm target by 2029.
players.read_note: The single most decision-relevant competitive fact for a mature-node foundry: a US$7.8B, 55k-wpm mature-node fab is being added in Singapore — the regional anchor — not in the other five. Overlaps the automotive/industrial/PMIC space a specialty foundry serves.

players.move: Large NAND front-end in Singapore; assembly/test footprint in the region.
players.read_note: Not foundry-comparable, but reinforces that front-end scale in the region sits in Singapore; adds regional power-demand context.

players.move: Kulim: world's largest 200mm SiC power fab, Phase 1 opened Aug 2024 (~€2B), Phase 2 up to €5B, up to 4,000 jobs, ~€5B design wins / ~€1B prepayments. Thailand: new back-end fab in Samut Prakan (first building ready early 2026); divested its older Bangkok/Nonthaburi back-end site to Malaysian Pacific Industries (completed Feb 2026) with a long-term supply agreement.
players.read_note: Confirms SEA's real specialty gravity is back-end and power/IDM manufacturing — not front-end foundry siting. Note (per revised appendix, now verified): Thailand is a back-end component base, not an advanced-packaging foundry.

players.location: Penang / Kulim (Malaysia); Ho Chi Minh City (Vietnam)
players.move: Advanced packaging and assembly/test expansion in Malaysia; large assembly/test site in Vietnam.
players.read_note: Confirms Malaysia and Vietnam as credible back-end / advanced-packaging locations.

players.move: US$1.6B campus (first two phases) at Yen Phong 2C; opened Oct 2023, first output Dec 2023, mass production from 2024; Advanced SiP and memory.
players.read_note: Anchors Vietnam's back-end credibility; a potential partner-ecosystem reference, not a competitor to front-end.

players.player: ASE / Inari / Unisem and Penang OSAT base
players.move: Established, deep OSAT and test cluster; Malaysia holds ~13% of global packaging/assembly/test activity (market/activity share, not physical capacity).
players.read_note: The strongest existing back-end ecosystem in the five — but this is mature packaging/test; advanced packaging is nascent (national target ~7% of global advanced packaging only by 2035). Relevant to partnership discussion, not to front-end siting.

synthesis: Confirmed front-end (300mm) additions cluster in Singapore — GlobalFoundries (US$4B, ~1.5M wpm) and VSMC (US$7.8B, ~55k wpm mature-node). The other five countries attract back-end, OSAT and power/IDM investment (Infineon Kulim SiC, Amkor Vietnam, Intel). For a specialty foundry, the front-end contest is a Singapore contest — and Singapore mature-node capacity is actively expanding, i.e. partly a crowding signal; the five-country opportunity, if any, is back-end adjacency and China+1 optionality.
